profiling_note: No runtime profiling data or Lighthouse reports were available. All findings are static-analysis inferences. Recommend EXPLAIN ANALYZE on flagged queries and a Lighthouse CI run for bundle metrics.

findings.title: SELECT * on ready_profiles — all profile columns transferred on every page load
findings.description: fetchProfile calls .select('*') on ready_profiles. The ReadyProfile type has 10 known columns, so this is low-overhead today, but any future wide column (e.g. a large JSONB blob) will silently inflate every authenticated page load.
findings.recommendation: Project only the columns consumed by the ReadyProfile interface: id, display_name, headline, goal, focus_areas, readiness_score, last_practice_date, assessments_completed, created_at, updated_at.
findings.fix_description: Replaced select('*') with explicit column list matching the ReadyProfile interface.

findings.title: 4 sequential Supabase round-trips in usePerformance.fetchPerformance
findings.description: fetchPerformance awaits four separate .from().select() calls sequentially: interview_practice_sessions, skill_gap_analyses, linkedin_profiles, portfolio_analyses. Each awaited call blocks the next, resulting in 4× serial network latency on every Dashboard and Playback render. On a 100ms p50 Supabase connection this adds ~300ms of avoidable wait.
findings.evidence:
const { data: practiceSessions } = await supabase.from('interview_practice_sessions')...
const { data: skillAnalyses } = await supabase.from('skill_gap_analyses')...
const { data: linkedin } = await supabase.from('linkedin_profiles')...
const { data: portfolio } = await supabase.from('portfolio_analyses')...
findings.recommendation: Dispatch all four queries concurrently with Promise.all(). Also replace select('*') on interview_practice_sessions and skill_gap_analyses with the minimum columns consumed (status, practice_data for sessions; gaps for skill analyses).
findings.fix_description: Wrapped all four queries in a single Promise.all(). Replaced select('*') on interview_practice_sessions with 'status, practice_data' and on skill_gap_analyses with 'gaps'. Added .limit(50) to interview_practice_sessions to bound the scan.

findings.title: SELECT * on interview_prep and interview_practice_sessions in PracticeCenter
findings.description: fetchData issues two sequential SELECT * queries with no LIMIT. interview_practice_sessions stores large JSONB blobs (questions array, practice_data array). Fetching all columns for an unbounded result set transfers O(sessions × blob_size) bytes on every visit to /practice.
findings.recommendation: Project required columns only, add LIMIT (e.g. 20 preps, 50 sessions), and run both queries in parallel with Promise.all().
findings.fix_description: Replaced select('*') with explicit column lists, added .limit(20) and .limit(50) respectively, and parallelised the two queries with Promise.all().

findings.title: SELECT * on interview_prep in PracticeSession
findings.description: fetchPrepAndStartSession fetches a single interview_prep row with SELECT *. The questions JSONB column can be large; only id, position, company, questions, and a few metadata columns are read downstream.
findings.recommendation: Select only the columns actually used: id, user_id, position, company, questions, created_at, updated_at.
findings.fix_description: Replaced select('*') with the explicit column list.

findings.title: SELECT * on linkedin_profiles and portfolio_analyses in Mirror
findings.description: Both fetchLinkedInAnalysis and fetchPortfolioAnalysis call SELECT * on their respective tables. The analysis_results JSONB can be several KB. All other columns beyond the ProfileRow interface fields are unused.
findings.recommendation: Project only the ProfileRow columns: id, user_id, linkedin_url / portfolio_url, analysis_results, created_at, updated_at, is_public, share_token.
findings.fix_description: Replaced both select('*') calls with explicit column projections matching the ProfileRow interface.

findings.title: SELECT * on skill_gap_analyses in SkillsGap page
findings.description: fetchPreviousAnalyses issues SELECT * on skill_gap_analyses. The table contains missing_skills, recommendations, and skill_gaps as JSONB columns that can be large arrays.
findings.recommendation: Project only the SkillGapAnalysis interface fields.
findings.fix_description: Replaced select('*') with 'id, user_id, target_role, missing_skills, recommendations, skill_gaps, created_at, updated_at'.

findings.title: SELECT * on learning_recommendations in LearningPaths page
findings.description: fetchSkillGapsAndRecommendations calls SELECT * on learning_recommendations. The resources JSONB can contain many resource objects.
findings.recommendation: Project only id, user_id, skill_gaps, resources, created_at.
findings.fix_description: Replaced select('*') with explicit column list.

findings.title: SELECT * on negotiation_sessions in NegotiationCoach
findings.description: fetchPreviousSessions calls SELECT * on negotiation_sessions. All NegotiationSession fields are consumed, but explicit projection guards against future column additions inflating the read path.
findings.recommendation: Project only the NegotiationSession interface columns.
findings.fix_description: Replaced select('*') with explicit NegotiationSession column list.

findings.title: SELECT * on career_narratives in readiness_score Netlify function
findings.description: The readiness_score function fetches all columns from career_narratives but only reads four JSONB fields (origin_story, pivot_explanation, value_proposition, future_vision) to compute narrativeScore. Any large narrative content is transferred unnecessarily.
findings.recommendation: Project only the four narrative fields that are actually tested.
findings.fix_description: Replaced select('*') with 'origin_story, pivot_explanation, value_proposition, future_vision'.

findings.title: No LIMIT on interview_sessions scan in get_practice_performance
findings.description: The get_practice_performance function fetches ALL interview_sessions for a user with no LIMIT. A power user with hundreds of sessions will cause a full table scan on the RLS-filtered partition, and the entire result set is loaded into Lambda memory. This is also the function called from the performance dashboard, so it runs on every page load.
findings.evidence: supabase.from('interview_sessions').select('id, score, question, created_at, interview_prep_id').eq('user_id', userId).order('created_at', { ascending: false }) — no .limit()
findings.recommendation: Add .limit(200) for the dashboard view. If historical aggregates are needed beyond 200 sessions, consider a separate aggregation job that persists rolled-up stats.

findings.title: Missing composite indexes inferred on heavily filtered tables
findings.description: Every data-access path filters by user_id, and several also ORDER BY created_at DESC or updated_at DESC. Without a composite index on (user_id, created_at DESC) / (user_id, updated_at DESC), Postgres must scan all rows matching user_id and then sort. Tables of concern: interview_practice_sessions, skill_gap_analyses, linkedin_profiles, portfolio_analyses, ai_invocations, readiness_snapshots.
findings.evidence:
Inferred from query patterns:
  .eq('user_id', ...).order('created_at', { ascending: false })
  .eq('user_id', ...).order('updated_at', { ascending: false })

questions.text: Do the Supabase migrations in supabase/ include composite indexes on (user_id, created_at) for interview_practice_sessions and skill_gap_analyses? This finding should be demoted if indexes already exist.

findings.title: In-memory AI response cache is never reused across Netlify function invocations
findings.description:
netlify/functions/ai/cache.ts maintains a module-level memoryCache Map. Netlify functions are ephemeral; the in-memory layer is cold on every cold-start (every new container spin-up). The Supabase ai_cache table is the only durable cache layer, but it is used as a write-through fallback, not as a primary read path. Every cache hit on a warm container that is then recycled requires a full DB round-trip on the next request.

Additionally, ai_cache is keyed on (task, input, tier, quality) — so two users requesting identical public content (e.g. the same skill-gap analysis prompt) generate separate cache entries, paying twice for the same AI call.
findings.recommendation:
1. Treat the Supabase cache table as the primary cache and always read from it first (already done in getCache). Remove the in-memory fallback or document that it only provides a micro-optimisation within the same container lifetime.
2. For shareable tasks (practice_question_gen, learn_skill_gap), consider a tier-agnostic cache key so identical prompts share a cached response across users.

findings.title: High-cost models (gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet) reachable by pro/premium tiers without per-session budget guardrail
findings.description: MODEL_REGISTRY lists gpt-4o at $0.50/1k tokens and claude-3-5-sonnet at $0.80/1k tokens. These are available to any pro or premium user. checkTierCap enforces daily call counts but not daily cost budgets. A single mirror_assessment or practice_evaluate call with a large transcript can consume 4–8k tokens, costing $3–6 per call. A premium user performing 1,000 high-quality calls per day (the tier's daily_cap) could generate up to $6,000/day in cost.
findings.recommendation: Add a daily cost budget cap alongside the call-count cap in checkTierCap. For example, track cumulative cost_estimate in ai_invocations and reject calls once the daily budget (e.g. $50 for premium) is exceeded. Also consider making max_tokens_hint task-specific and conservative for standard-quality calls.

findings.title: Full ai_usage table scan without time bound in cost-tracking utility
findings.description: cost-tracking.ts issues supabase.from('ai_usage').select('cost, provider').eq('user_id', userId) without any time filter for the 'all-time' aggregate. As ai_usage grows, this query will become increasingly expensive and slow.
findings.evidence: const allResponse = await supabase.from('ai_usage').select('cost, provider').eq('user_id', userId);
findings.recommendation: Either cap the all-time aggregate at a reasonable horizon (e.g. 1 year) or maintain a pre-aggregated total_cost column on the user profile updated via a DB trigger, eliminating the full table scan.

findings.title: Dashboard ReadinessScore renders with hardcoded score=78 — never fetches real data
findings.description: The Dashboard passes score={78} as a static prop to ReadinessScore. There is no data-fetch on mount, so the displayed score never reflects the user's actual readiness. This is likely a placeholder, but it means the readiness_score Netlify function (which performs 5+ DB queries) is never called from the main dashboard, and users see stale/incorrect data.
findings.recommendation: Connect ReadinessScore to real data: either call the readiness_score endpoint on mount or expose the score from the usePerformance hook (which already calculates it).

findings.title: No route-level code splitting configured in Vite
findings.description: All page components are imported statically in the router. With 18 page components — several pulling in large inline CSS and per-route data hooks — the initial JS bundle will contain code for pages the user has not yet visited. No dynamic import() or React.lazy() wrappers are present.
findings.evidence:
vite.config.ts has no manual rollupOptions.output.manualChunks config.
Page imports in src/App.tsx or router file appear to be static (inferred — no lazy() calls found in src/).
findings.recommendation: Wrap route components in React.lazy() and Suspense. Vite will automatically split them into separate async chunks, reducing the initial parse cost for first-time visitors.

findings.title: openai npm package in client-side dependencies
findings.description: The openai package (v6) is listed in dependencies, not devDependencies. Although src/ does not currently import from 'openai' directly (the AI calls go through Netlify functions), the package will be available for accidental client-side import and may be included in vendor chunks if any bundler plugin resolves it. The package is 500+ KB unpacked.
findings.evidence: "openai": "^6.15.0" listed in dependencies (not devDependencies). No import from 'openai' found in src/.
findings.recommendation: Move openai to devDependencies, or better, to a dedicated netlify/functions/package.json that is never bundled by Vite. This prevents accidental client inclusion and reduces install size for the frontend CI step.

questions.text: Are composite indexes on (user_id, created_at DESC) present in the Supabase migrations for the flagged tables? Please run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the queries listed in PERF-011 and share the output to confirm or dismiss this finding.

What is the observed Netlify function cold-start rate for the ai/* functions in production? If cold starts are rare (warm containers reused >90% of the time), the in-memory cache provides real value and the finding severity should be lowered.
What is the current average daily AI cost per premium user? This will confirm whether the cost cap finding is theoretical or an active concern.

summary.estimated_data_transfer_reduction: Significant reduction for users with large JSONB blobs in interview_practice_sessions and skill_gap_analyses
